metadata.title: GlobalFoundries (GFS) — the AMD spinout, Mubadala ownership, the 7nm retreat + IBM suit, CHIPS money, and the DoD 'trusted foundry' (both sides, dated)
metadata.status: the AMD spinout, Chartered merger, Mubadala majority ownership, the 2018 7nm exit, the IBM $1.5B handover + $2.5B lawsuit, the 2021 IPO, the ~$1.5B CHIPS award, and the DoD trusted-foundry accreditation are FACT (SEC filings, court records, DOC/CHIPS, GF disclosures); the 'strategic US asset' vs 'foreign-sovereign-owned trusted foundry' tension is presented BOTH ways with dates
metadata.frame: Deeper dig on GlobalFoundries (already a node via the PsiQuantum photonic-chip tie). GF is the third-largest pure-play foundry and a rare US-based volume fab operator — but majority-owned by Abu Dhabi's sovereign fund (Mubadala), and it deliberately EXITED the leading edge in 2018 to focus on specialty/mature nodes. It is also a DoD-accredited 'trusted foundry' and a CHIPS Act recipient. The block maps: the AMD spinout + Chartered merger, the Mubadala ownership (cross-linking the Gulf-capital cluster + MGX), the 7nm retreat and the resulting IBM lawsuit, and the trusted-foundry/CHIPS national-security layer. Attaches to GlobalFoundries, AMD, IBM, US_Government, MGX, and the semiconductor supply-chain thread.

the_origin: GlobalFoundries was created in 2009 when AMD spun out its manufacturing operations (going fabless), funded by Abu Dhabi's Advanced Technology Investment Company (ATIC, later folded into Mubadala); in 2010 GF merged with Singapore's Chartered Semiconductor. It became the third-largest pure-play foundry (after TSMC and Samsung) and IPO'd on NASDAQ (GFS) in Oct 2021 at a ~$47B valuation — with Mubadala remaining the majority owner. grade: fact.
the_7nm_retreat: In Aug 2018, under CEO Tom Caulfield, GF ABANDONED its 7nm leading-edge roadmap, judging the capital cost of competing with TSMC/Samsung/Intel at the bleeding edge uneconomic. It pivoted to DIFFERENTIATED specialty/mature nodes: RF, FD-SOI (FDX), automotive, power, and silicon photonics. Both sides: critics saw the US ceding leading-edge logic; GF argued specialty foundry is a more defensible, profitable niche (and it has been profitable). grade: fact (the exit); 'retreat vs smart focus' is the read.
the_ibm_suit: GF's fab footprint partly came from IBM: in 2015 IBM PAID GF ~$1.5B to TAKE OVER its chip-manufacturing (East Fishkill, NY + Essex Junction, VT fabs) plus IP/engineers. After GF dropped 7nm, IBM sued GF (2021) for ~$2.5B, alleging GF failed to deliver the 7nm/10nm chips IBM had relied on for its servers; the parties SETTLED later in 2021. grade: fact (the 2015 deal + the 2021 suit/settlement).
the_chips_and_trusted_foundry: The national-security layer: GF is a DoD-accredited 'TRUSTED FOUNDRY' / secure US supplier (the Essex Junction, VT fab does RF/defense work) and it won a ~$1.5B CHIPS Act direct-funding award (Feb 2024) to expand Malta, NY (Fab 8) and Burlington, VT for automotive, defense, and aerospace chips. So Washington is subsidizing a US fab footprint precisely for supply-chain + defense security. grade: fact.
the_two_sides: Both ways, dated: (A) STRATEGIC-ASSET framing — GF is one of very few US-based volume foundries and a DoD trusted supplier; CHIPS money is rational supply-chain + defense insurance, and specialty nodes (auto/RF/defense) are exactly where onshore capacity matters. (B) FOREIGN-OWNERSHIP concern — a US 'trusted foundry' making defense chips is MAJORITY-OWNED by a foreign sovereign wealth fund (Mubadala/Abu Dhabi), and the 2018 7nm exit means the US still depends on TSMC/Samsung for leading-edge logic. Both are real and dated; neither is adopted as the verdict. grade: both documented.
the_honest_reading: GlobalFoundries is a genuinely strategic but ambivalent asset: a rare US volume foundry and DoD trusted supplier that Washington is subsidizing (CHIPS) for security — yet majority-owned by an Abu Dhabi sovereign fund and deliberately OUT of the leading edge since 2018. Its fab base came partly from IBM (the $1.5B 2015 handover) and produced a $2.5B IBM lawsuit when GF dropped 7nm. The dated facts (ownership, the 7nm exit, the IBM suit, the CHIPS award, the trusted-foundry status) sit alongside an unresolved both-sides tension between 'critical US capacity' and 'foreign-sovereign-owned defense supplier.' Cross-links the Gulf-capital cluster (Mubadala/MGX) and the semiconductor supply chain. Overlay; excluded from the proofs.

sources.name: GlobalFoundries — Wikipedia (AMD spinout, Chartered, Mubadala, 7nm exit, IPO)
CNBC — IBM sues GlobalFoundries for $2.5B over abandoned 7nm chips (2021)
US Dept of Commerce / CHIPS — GlobalFoundries ~$1.5B award (Feb 2024, Malta NY + Burlington VT)

edges.raw_instrument: majority owner (via ATIC, 2009-; ~80%+ at IPO) — Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund; funded the AMD spinout + 2010 Chartered merger; IPO Oct 2021 (NASDAQ GFS, ~$47B)
GF spun out of AMD's manufacturing (2009); AMD went fabless; legacy wafer-supply agreement
IBM PAID GF ~$1.5B (2015) to take over its fabs (East Fishkill NY + Essex Junction VT); after GF dropped 7nm, IBM SUED GF ~$2.5B (2021) for failing to deliver 7nm/10nm — settled 2021
CHIPS and Science Act (2022): federal semiconductor manufacturing incentives
~$1.5B direct CHIPS award (Feb 2024) to expand Malta NY (Fab 8) + Burlington VT for auto/defense/aerospace chips
DoD-accredited 'trusted foundry' / secure US chip manufacturing (Essex Junction VT RF/defense)
Mubadala (Abu Dhabi SWF) is a backer of MGX, the Gulf AI-investment vehicle — same sovereign-capital cluster
